turns.content: I'm getting this error when deserializing: serde::de::Error { kind: SerdeErrorKind::UnknownField("api_version", line: 5, column: 12) }. I tried using deny_unknown_fields but it seems to block everything instead of keeping the unknown data. What went wrong?

The error comes from serde::de::Error when you use deny_unknown_fields on a struct because that attribute tells the deserializer to panic or skip entirely if it encounters any field not explicitly defined in your struct. If you want to capture extra fields instead, you need to change your approach by using an enum variant like UnknownFields with a HashMap<String, Value>. You should define a newtype wrapper struct around that inner map so serde can recognize the whole thing as a single expected field.

Ah okay, so I need to wrap it in a specific struct. Can you show me exactly how to declare this unknown fields container and attach it to my main data struct without breaking the existing serde attributes?

You should define a newtype wrapper like UnknownFields(HashMap<String, Value>) so that serde can treat it as one cohesive field. Then in your main struct, declare an attribute deny_unknown_fields on the inner HashMap instead of trying to use it directly on the outer struct. This way, when extra fields appear during deserialization, they get captured into the map rather than causing a failure.
Wait, I just realized something else - what happens if I have multiple unknown fields at different lines? Does serde still work correctly or does it stop after the first one?

Serde will continue deserializing through all remaining fields regardless of how many unknown ones appear earlier in the input. The only constraint is that your main struct's fields must match exactly what you expect, while everything else gets funneled into the unknown-fields map. This means you can safely deserialize large payloads with random extra keys without breaking your core data extraction logic.

That makes sense. But I'm still worried about schema drift over time - if someone sends an old version of my API and I only expect new fields, how do I ensure the unknown fields collection doesn't silently accept dangerous data?

You can validate the contents of that captured map after deserialization by checking if any unexpected keys appear in your schema. Since serde captures everything into a HashMap<String, Value>, you can iterate through its values and compare them against known types or run custom validation logic on each field before it reaches your application layer.
